input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, where the first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with a gear transmission followed by a cycloidal gear, and the robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction through the integration of white-box and black-box models to enhance motion accuracy, and integrated visualization capabilities within a unified dashboard for real-time monitoring and analysis of the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tooling and enablers encompass Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for managing abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are utilized to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. System behavior is described via MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, with detailed behavior handled using MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models utilize Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies with SysML for system structure and behavior, Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) for component & connector perspectives, and behavioral descriptions via MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines. These models are integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and code in C++/Java for detailed behavior analysis. Data components consist of contextual data traces from physical systems, aggregated and abstracted via digital shadow data flows, processed by the DT's active components for interpretation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series data from physical sensors (e.g., input current and output angle) are streamed via MQTT protocol; data preprocessing includes computing output torque using a dynamics model.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming sensor data between physical and digital components; network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability mechanisms, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle includes development phases characterized by hierarchical abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer) as defined in Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), transitioning to operational phases where the DT actively processes physical system data. Lifecycle governance integrates heterogeneous stakeholders through methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development process employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer), utilizing methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is achieved through MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, and integration with tools like MathWorks Simulink, simulation tools, and general-purpose languages (C++, Java).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML [17], ACME [23], AADL [24], and MontiArc [25, 26] for architecture description and systems engineering modeling, facilitating component & connector perspectives and interoperability across heterogeneous modeling tools and stakeholders.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the physical system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and provides detailed specifications of the first axis, including the 'permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission', and 'cycloidal gear'. It also correctly identifies the controller as the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'. All details are explicitly supported by Section III of the source text.

The extracted description correctly summarizes the primary services described in the Abstract and Introduction. It includes the 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', the use of 'white-box and black-box models' to 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy', and the 'visualization in a dashboard' for integrated analysis.

The extraction is highly detailed and accurately reflects the 'Modeling Software and Systems' section of the source text. It correctly lists the MBSE methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT), Architecture Description Languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs), and specific tools like MathWorks Simulink and programming languages C++ and Java.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents focus on robot motion control, friction modeling, and the implementation of digital shadows. There is no mention of data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, or consent management. The text highlights the goal of combining models with live data: 'investigate the research question how analytical... and data-driven... friction models can be effectively combined'.

The documents mention a physical 'safety cell' that restricts the robot's working range, but they do not address cybersecurity protocols, access control, authentication, or risk assessment approaches as required by the characteristic definition. The focus remains on data linking and accuracy: 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'.
